customer_anxiety_profile: Fixed-income widow, approximately 70+, living alone in a home she has occupied for roughly 20 years. Primary anxiety is cost — she opened the call by saying she probably cannot afford solar. She agreed to the appointment but her emotional buy-in to the value proposition was not built on this call. The closer should expect to re-establish the financial case from the beginning.
unresolved_concerns: Fixed-income affordability: Candy stated 'I'm on a fixed income' and 'I don't probably can't afford it' at the top of the call. The bill-swap framing was introduced but never emotionally anchored. The closer needs to lead with the cost-replacement framing, not a new-expense framing.

Bill composition complexity: Candy's monthly bill includes electric, fiber optic internet, a $20 phone line for her home security landline, and propane — all on one consolidated statement. The closer should be prepared for a bill that is not a simple electric-only figure.

Fiber optic bill discrepancy: Candy gave two different fiber optic amounts — $99 at 03:03 ('fiber optic's 99') and $20 at 09:35 ('my fiber optic's still 20'). It is possible the $20 figure refers to the phone line and the $99 to internet, but this was not clarified on the call. The closer should confirm the actual breakdown before building the design.
sensitive_topics_raised: Spousal loss: Candy mentioned her husband lived in the home since it was built in 1968 and has since died. She did not dwell on it, but the disclosure was unprompted. The closer should be aware of this context — Candy is likely making this decision alone.
Fixed income / financial vulnerability: Candy explicitly identified herself as being on a fixed income. Any framing that sounds like a new monthly expense will trigger resistance.
exact_promises_rep_made: Appointment booked for the following day (the 20th) at 1PM Ohio time — phone call format.
Bucky will send a text reminder before the appointment.
The specialist will call Candy at 1PM — Candy does not need to call in.
closer_should_know: Candy's emotional buy-in was not built on the setter call — she agreed to the appointment but the fixed-income cost anxiety was never resolved and neither bill-pain signal was flipped. The closer must lead with the cost-replacement framing (solar replaces the bill, not adds to it) before moving to design. The 25.69% year-over-year bill increase Candy read aloud is a live leverage point the closer can use.
